Here's a comparison of Northern Dynasty with Seabridge Gold Inc.'s ((SA) KSM project and NovaGold Resources Inc.'s (NG) Donlin project.

Risks

The risk list here is long and well-documented.

First, you have the EPA veto, which remains in effect, and I think a favorable court ruling still is no guarantee of a permit for Pebble.

Second, the company is in pre-production and therefore pre-revenue with no cash flow. It's burning a lot of cash, and its auditors have flagged a going-concern risk.

The stock is heavily traded by momentum and retail speculators, which creates volatility that has nothing to do with fundamentals. And the $3.1 billion capex requirement, in a remote Alaskan location without existing infrastructure, would require a partner that no major miner has been willing to be since the early 2010s.

Bottom Line

If this project were located almost anywhere else, it would be one of the most exciting development-stage assets in the world. But it isn't located somewhere else, unfortunately, sitting at the headwaters of the most productive wild salmon fishery on the planet. From what I've researched over the years, every level of government, the courts, environmental groups, Alaska Native communities, and the fishing industry have lined up against it.

Northern Dynasty is spending roughly ~$9 million per year on legal and corporate costs to keep this fight alive. The stock has become a momentum trade, not an investment in a future mine. I am not saying it can't work. But I am saying the probability-weighted outcome does not justify the risk when alternatives like Seabridge Gold exist in the same space with far fewer obstacles.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: The Math On What's A Fair Downside
data.description:
Business Brief

Blue Owl Capital (OWL) is a private equity [PE] firm. It has $307B in AUM split across Credit (lending to mid-large private companies), Real Assets (owning and collecting rents on, or lending against hard assets like real estate or data centers) and GP Strategic Capital (buying minority stakes in other investment firms). Here's the AUM mix:

Blue Owl Capital earns management and sometimes performance fees on its fee-paying AUM (FPAUM). About 61% of its total AUM is earning fees as of the Dec'25 quarter:

Permanent capital in a PE manager like OWL refers to money that does not have a fixed end date at which the principal must be returned to investors. So in theory, this capital can stay with a PE manager indefinitely, earning steady management and performance fees every year. So having a high portion of permanent capital improves the quality and stability of the PE manager's earnings. Blue Owl Capital has about 73% of its total AUM as permanent capital:

Elevator Pitch

Blue Owl Capital is a very interesting situation because from a historical financials print perspective, nothing seems wrong. But the market has been strongly reacting to expectations of future weakness. Here's how I'm making sense of it:

  1. Historical growth and margin trends look good
  2. Concerns on portfolio value is driving fear-driven selloffs in the stock
  3. Low-ball offers from activist investors and high short interest make a case for an upside squeeze
  4. OWL stock is being de-rated but perhaps justifiably so
  5. Don't catch the falling knife

Historical Growth and Margin Trends Look Good

The fee-related revenues and fee-related earnings (FRE) margins are growing nicely and are at healthy levels:

AUM growth is also in a good spot with >20% YoY growth:

Concerns on Portfolio Value is Driving Fear-Driven Selloffs in the Stock

In my recent article on the Ares Capital (ARCC) business development company [BDC], I discussed the issue of loans with software exposure. I recommend reading that article for more context as a similar theme applies here to Blue Owl Capital.

Blue Owl Capital's loan exposure to the software sector across its entire AUM is about 8%. Last month, the Glendon Capital Management investment firm claimed that Blue Owl Capital and other PE firms' actual loss rates may be higher than reported. The firm believes that Blue Owl is marking the riskier junior software debt at a 10% loss (90 cents on the dollar) and the supposedly much safer, senior debt was trading publicly at rates that implied a loss of 22% (78 cents on the dollar). If this analysis is accurate, that can imply material NAV erosion for some of its funds.

And this kind of news along with the broader uncertainty on the AI disruption risk to software has triggered large redemption requests to the tune of 22% and 41% of outstanding shares for the Blue Owl Credit Income Corp (OCIC) and Blue Owl Technology Income Corp (OTIC) BDCs. But redemption limits are capped at 5% per quarter.

Now, the net outflows in these funds at the 5% redemption limits lead to a small 1-2% NAV erosion for these arguably flagship funds for Blue Owl Capital as they have been described as "evergreen wealth products" that contribute to the permanent capital base for Blue Owl Capital. However, I believe the bigger hit and the driver of the 46% drawdown in the stock this year is the break in the narrative that the permanent capital base may not be as reliable a source of steady earnings growth as that is being whittled down by redemptions.

Low-Ball Offers From Activist Investors and High Short Interest Make a Case for an Upside Squeeze

The negative sentiment on OWL has spiked the stock's short interest to 17.87%. Typically, I consider any level above 10% as a candidate for a short squeeze. I believe another sentiment indicator for upside is the company's recommendation to reject the offers of activist investors such as Saba Capital and Cox Capital to acquire the 3 key BDCs (Blue Owl Technology Income Corp, Blue Owl Credit Income Corp and Blue Owl Capital Corporation II) at prices 20-35% below net asset value (NAV). Communication from Blue Owl Capital and its funds has explicitly stated that the low-ball offers are much below fair value:

Cox and Saba's offer price is inadequate, arbitrary, and substantially undervalues OBDC II's assets and ongoing access to liquidity... We believe this is an attempt to capture value at the expense of OBDC II shareholders. The Board strongly recommends that shareholders REJECT Cox and Saba's unsolicited, minority tender offer and DO NOT tender their shares.

- Blue Owl Capital

I am inclined to assign some weight to management's reassurance especially because they have a reasonable track record in meeting expectations as you can see in the normalized PAT margin surprise vs consensus estimates:

Regulatory Scrutiny is Another Headwind

US regulators are getting increasingly concerned about the relative lack of transparency in mark-to-market private security valuations, liquidity concerns and the lack of sufficient guardrails to ensure prudent loan portfolio growth. So they are starting to have a closer eye on the private credit sector.

For example, lawmakers led by the Democrats have sent inquiries to many large PE firms including Blue Owl Capital to understand various aspects of their business practices:

The inquiries cover areas such as sales practices, leverage, fees, incentives, audits, risk management, and broader economic vulnerabilities

- Seeking Alpha News

And the US Treasury is proactively engaging with the insurance industry to ensure that stresses in the $2T non-banking lending sector and broader private equity markets do not cascade to the broader economy as it did in the Global Financial Crisis:

We want to gauge, could it have any effects on the overall economy? Thus far, it's been very additive, but again, how does it affect the regulated system? And we want to prevent contagion...

...the Trump administration would not allow working Americans' savings and investment accounts to become "a dumping ground" for "rotten" assets.

- Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent

I expect this to be a short term headwind for Blue Owl Capital and its peers as it can raise compliance costs and slow down growth. We should keep an eye on the G&A intensity and the overall fundraising growth trends to monitor for these effects:

OWL Stock is Being De-Rated but Probably to an Excessive Extent

OWL has traded at a 16.4% discount vs peers on average over the past year. But now, it finds itself at a very steep 35.2% discount vs its alternative asset manager comps:

I have taken the companies in the VanEck Alternative Asset Manager ETF (GPZ) to form the peer set in the analysis above.

Now, if this was a mere case of multiple contraction, it would signal clear undervaluation for OWL. Unfortunately, that is not the case as the fundamental earnings expectations index has also tanked down:

You can see how in the last couple of months, Wall St sell-side have put in unanimous downgrades to normalized EPS estimates for all quarters of FY26:

But let's now try to approach this from a more bottom-up perspective to understand how much of the selloff may be justified:

Currently, OCIC and OTIC AUMs are $34.2B and $5.1B. Taking into account the 22% and 41% of outstanding shares redemption requests, that implies OCIC and OTIC AUM hits of $7.5B and $2.1B respectively for a total AUM hit of $9.6B.

Now, OWL's annual fee-related management fees as a mix of FPAUM is 1.4%:

Taking that figure, I get an overall OCIC and OTIC FPAUM management fees hit of $135M, which is 5.3% of OWL's total annual FPAUM management fee of $2.5B. Assuming the EV/FPAUM management fee multiple remains unchanged, this means a fair EV downside of 5.3%.

Now based on the OWL's current market capitalization of $6.6B and EV of $14.1B, the implied net debt is $7.5B. So using this, we can work through the fair downside hit to market capitalization of $5.8B, implying a 11.4% fair downside to the stock. Here's the summary of the math:

However, the stock has obviously corrected a lot more than this. So from this perspective, OWL's sell-off seems overdone.

Don't Catch the Falling Knife

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

OWL Technical Analysis

The monthly chart of OWL shows some very strong bearish price action with high volumes as well. I don't see any sign of buyers. I think it is prudent to respect the trend and not go knife-catching against this selling pressure.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Neutral as a lot of the bearish reasons are expectations-driven and there is no evidence of deterioration in the key AUM growth, FRE revenues and FRE margin numbers yet.
  • Sentiment Analysis: Neutral as the upside risk from a potential short seller squeeze and management's recommendation to reject low-ball tender offers are offset by increased regulatory scrutiny.
  • Valuation: Bullish as my bottom-up math suggests the >40% sell-off is overdone.
  • Technical Analysis: Bearish due to strong bearish momentum and not much signs of buyers
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Neutral based on my judgement and synthesis of the different analysis types

data.title: Microsoft: Claude Just Threatened Copilot Adoption
data.description:
2026 has not started well for big tech investors. Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) has been the hardest-hit big tech company so far this year, with its stock down ~21% YTD. In fact, Q1 marked the worst performance for MSFT since 2008. This lackluster performance came on the back of rising investor skepticism surrounding aggressive AI investments (CapEx projected to hit $146 billion in FY 2026) and growth fears for the cloud business. As a long-term shareholder, I have remained optimistic during this downturn as I continue to believe in the long-term growth potential of the company.

Last week, an interesting development came to light with Anthropic launching the beta version of Claude for Word. This is a native sidebar add-in developed specifically for Microsoft Word. Claude has been aggressively rolling out new features in the past month or so (Claude Cowork general availability, persistent memory, managed agents, multi-agent review, etc.), and this beta version of Claude for Word is its newest product rollout. Claude for Word directly threatens to displace Copilot, which is the product suite developed by Microsoft in partnership with OpenAI's ChatGPT (Microsoft owns a key stake in OpenAI).

Amid the growing risk of Copilot revenue displacement, I am taking a more cautious stance on Microsoft today.

Claude for Word

To get a quick understanding of Claude for Word, I highly recommend watching this official release video published by Claude.

For now, the beta version of this Word add-in is available to Claude Team and Enterprise users. I do not have a Claude subscription, but I tried Claude for Word on a colleague's laptop. I noticed several features in this add-in that make a real difference for Word users.

  1. Native integration of tracked changes (this is a very nice feature that could save a lot of time for enterprise users and behaves like a human collaborator monitoring the work).
  2. Preservation of complex formatting within a document.
  3. Drafting assistance within the context of a document.

If you have ever used Copilot within the Office suite, you'd agree with me when I say that Copilot has much deeper integration capability with Office apps. So it should be, given that Microsoft is an investor in OpenAI. However, at a practical level, Claude for Word offers a superior experience to users because of how it works. Let me give you an example based on what I tried over the past weekend to understand the hype behind Claude for Word.

If you are using Copilot within Word, when Copilot is used to rewrite a section within a document, what it does is replace the existing text altogether. For the entire rewritten block of content, you'd see an approve or reject button. If you use Claude for Word for the same task, what happens is that Claude natively integrates with the tracked changes feature within Word. Rather than blindly replacing the text, Claude for Word highlights in red the changes it made, including the deleted words. A user has the option to revert any of these individual changes. This offers much more functionality than if you were prompted to approve or reject an entire block of a document.

I tested the comment tracking feature and also the formatting features as well. Although I am not planning to dive deeper into why Claude for Word offers a better experience (I'll leave you to experiment), I can conclusively remark that Claude for Word offers a much better user experience based on my experiments over the weekend.

Understanding How Claude for Word Could Threaten Microsoft's Growth

Claude for Word is not just a standalone add-in. Before this, Anthropic launched Claude for Excel and Claude for PowerPoint as well. Today, these add-ins can sort of speak to each other and handle tasks across platforms. Before you jump to any conclusions, I am not going to claim that Claude will erase the Office apps from existence. The MS Office suite is going nowhere. However, as a long-term shareholder of Microsoft, I see potential monetization problems beginning to surface if the company does not make progress with advancing Copilot.

First, we have to understand the important role ARPU growth is expected to play in Microsoft's revenue growth in the future. According to recent remarks by CFO Amy Hood, the paid Microsoft 365 subscriber base is around 450 million seats today. Independently, Medha Cloud estimates Microsoft's overall market share at just over 45%.

Exhibit 1: Microsoft 365 user stats

Seat growth will be slow from here. This is because Microsoft has already captured a very large share of the enterprise market. Going forward, I believe Microsoft's growth will largely come from ARPU growth. For this to happen, the company has to focus on pricing, not just volume. Microsoft's Copilot strategy is to sell it as an add-on priced at $30 per seat.

Exhibit 2: Microsoft enterprise packages

A $30 subscription to Copilot, if successfully marketed, can boost Microsoft's ARPU meaningfully. But the problem is that a $30 add-on seems material given the low base prices for the enterprise licenses. For an enterprise user currently paying around $36 for an E3 license, paying an additional $30/month just for Copilot marks an 80%+ price hike. If Microsoft were to be successful with this pricing strategy in the long run, Copilot needs to be the best in its class. For now, even the beta version of Claude for Word seems to be doing a better job at context-heavy editing.

This is where the Claude risk becomes evident. Given that Claude products have already made their way into the IT departments of many global companies, Claude subscriptions have skyrocketed. Claude for Word and other Office add-ins are available to all Claude Teams and Enterprise members (subscriptions starting at $25/month).

Claude for Word is targeted at enterprise users who deal with large documents, such as financial analysts and legal professionals. If we do not see any material improvement in the way Microsoft Copilot interacts with users, I believe IT departments that already pay for Claude will find it very difficult to justify spending an additional $30/month per seat for Copilot. This makes ARPU growth challenging for Microsoft at a time when the company is banking on upselling subscriptions to grow revenue.

Based on Q2 FY2026 data, there were 15 million active paid Copilot seats. Based on the total Microsoft 365 subscriber count of around 450 million, this represents a penetration rate of just over 3%. An alarming statistic is that only around 36% of employees with access to Copilot actually used it in the past quarter.

Given that many analysts have baked in notable success for Copilot in their earnings models for Microsoft, I ran a sensitivity analysis to project the revenue shortfall that we have to deal with if penetration falls short of expectations. The below table summarizes my findings.

Metric Value
Total addressable market 450 million commercial seats for Microsoft 365
Copilot subscription fee $30/month
Wall Street analyst consensus for TAM penetration 35%
Expected ARR based on consensus penetration $56.7 billion
Expected ARR if penetration drops to 20% $32.4 billion
Annual revenue shortfall $24.3 billion

Source: Author's estimates

In my sensitivity analysis above, I modeled the Copilot penetration to drop from an expected 35% to just 20% if the adoption of Claude for Office add-ins forces IT departments to abandon their plans to upgrade to Copilot plans. Again, I expect this to happen only among high-value users of Microsoft 365 products.

If a revenue shortfall materializes, we are looking at the possibility of an earnings multiple compression. The Productivity and Business Processes segment of the company is valued on expectations for high-margin SaaS revenue growth. Any shortfall here, in my opinion, will directly impact the company's valuation. A $24 billion potential revenue shortfall cannot be taken lightly. Because of this, I will keep a close eye on Microsoft's Copilot pricing strategy and product updates to determine if the company can negate this threat in the second half of FY2026.

Q3 Earnings Preview

Microsoft is expected to report Q3 FY2026 earnings on April 29. With less than 2 weeks to the earnings print, I am keeping an eye on a few data points.

Revenue for the quarter is expected to be $81.39 billion, representing YoY growth of ~16%. The Intelligent Cloud segment and the PBP segment are expected to account for ~85% of total revenue in Q3. The consensus EPS for the quarter is $4.07.

I will keep an eye on these headline numbers, but my main focus will be on a few other data points that could help me evaluate the growth potential of the company better.

  • I will monitor the spread between Microsoft 365 commercial cloud revenue growth and commercial seat growth to gauge whether Copilot subscriptions are expanding as expected. If Copilot growth is failing to offset the negative impact of saturating seat growth, it will be a red flag.
  • In Q2 FY2026, Microsoft reported 160% YoY growth in Copilot seats to around 15 million. In this quarter, my focus will be on QoQ growth for Copilot seats to determine if there is any momentum behind Copilot adoption.
  • On the upcoming earnings call, the management will most likely discuss the drivers behind E5 license growth. Ideally, I would want Copilot adoption to be the biggest driver.
  • I will also be looking for movements in RPO, which stood at $625 billion at the end of Q2, to understand if RPO growth is dominated by long-term contract wins or by short-term billings. If the latter comes true, it could turn out to be a signal of Copilot strength.
  • As many other Seeking Alpha analysts have discussed, it is important to keep an eye on Microsoft's CapEx plans as well. The market is increasingly becoming skeptical of aggressive AI CapEx.

Takeaway

I have been bullish on Microsoft in the past 8+ years. I last covered it in 2025. The company has delivered on its promises, and I am quite content with the gains I am sitting on. That said, I don't feel comfortable adding to my position today because of the threat faced by Copilot adoption at a time when ARPU growth is expected to drive future revenue growth for the company. Given this risk and growing CapEx, I have decided to take a more cautious stance on MSFT. I have no plans to book my gains just yet, but I will not be adding to my position before I have more clarity on Copilot growth as well.

data.title: SPX Technologies: Data Center Part Of The Business Is Attractive
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a buy rating for SPX Technologies (SPXC) because I think it is entering a phase where its HVAC growth profile can improve meaningfully, driven by rising exposure to data center cooling and backed by expanding capacity. My view is that the data center piece is becoming large enough to move the needle, and the market is not rewarding SPXC the right multiple when compared to peers.

Company Description

SPXC operates two segments: (1) HVAC and (2) Detection & Measurement [DNM]. HVAC is the larger business, where SPXC designs, manufactures, installs, and services all related products and solutions, like packaged and process cooling products, engineered air movement and handling solutions, and hydronic and electrical heating and ventilation products. SPXC serves multiple end markets, including industrial, institutional, commercial, and residential end markets. For DNM, SPXC sells underground pipe and cable locators, inspection and rehabilitation equipment, robotic systems, transportation systems, communication technologies, and also aids to navigation products. In terms of sales channels, HVAC sells through direct channels, independent representatives, distributors, and retailers, while DNM sells mainly direct and through distributors.

Data Center Cooling Story

The main reason I am bullish on SPXC is because the data center cooling story is starting to get really tangible now. Per SPXC disclosure, data center revenue in 2025 was a little more than $200 million, or ~9% of company revenue. Management also said that mix should move into the low double digits in 2026, with data center sales growing in the 50% range. Assuming the rest of the business is flat, this implies the data center sales mix to be ~13% in 2026, and at the rate of 50%/year, it would snowball to a size of >30% by the fourth year. In other words, this is no longer a side business; this aspect of SPXC story is becoming large enough to move the needle.

Olympus Max is the best proof that this growth can continue. This is SPXC’s new product that can run either dry, using no water, or in adiabatic mode, which gives customers more thermal performance when needed. As you can imagine, the practical point here is flexibility. Customers can optimize between water and power usage, and management also said the dry unit can be upgraded to adiabatic later. I strongly believe this matters a lot because it pushes SPXC deeper into the cooling market, enabling it to serve a pool of demand that it couldn’t previously. We know to a good extent this product works, as SPXC had targeted $50 million of bookings for Olympus Max in 2025 and did reach that target (those bookings now convert into revenue in 2026). Management even went ahead to say that they have already won material business with three customers and that one customer has locked up multiple years of growing demand.

If you take a step back, this product traction matters even more when we look at the broader direction of the market. My view is that the data center market is moving toward more water-cooled chilling architectures as compute intensity rises. As such, more of the data center budgets are going to be allocated to this part of the market that SPXC is positioning itself to be in. While there is still a timing risk (that is, when all this demand ramps up), I think the evidence is now tangible enough to treat this as a real growth driver.

Having a solid demand tailwind is good, but having the capacity to meet that demand is just as important. Even if a product gets early traction, if the manufacturing footprint is not there, growth will not come through. But that is not the case for SPXC, as it is already ramping up its footprint. One good example is the new 459,000-square-foot facility in Madison, Alabama. That site will support custom air handling and data center solutions, including Olympus Max. The timeline for assembly capability to come online is in H2 2026, with initial production in H1 2027.

Valuation

SPXC valuation has trended up over the past few years, from low teens during the pre-covid era to the current 27x NTM PE. If we look at the past 10 years, one might think that SPXC is expensive because the average is ~18x. But I don’t think 10 years is the right timeline. Given the shift in underlying demand for HVAC, especially due to the growing build-out of data centers, I think the past 3 to 5 years is the right timeframe to anchor SPXC’s current valuation. In this case, the average is 22x, and SPXC is trading close to the 1.5 standard deviation level.

Given that this industry has plenty of other mature companies, we can easily benchmark SPXC’s valuation to these peers. For example, peers such as Trane Technologies (TT) trade at 30x (with ~10% two-year revenue CAGR), and Johnson Controls (JCI) trades at 26.5x with high-single-digit two-year revenue CAGR. Given that SPXC has low-teens two-year revenue CAGR (based on consensus estimates), I won’t be surprised if it trades at a TT valuation of 30x.

From SPXC's earnings history, we know that consensus has been fairly accurate in their estimates. Taking ~$10.56 in FY2029 and multiplying by 30x, I can bridge to a share price target of ~$317, or ~44% upside.

Risk

As this data center part of the business is becoming the main growth driver, the risk is SPXC may not win as much demand as I thought because there are strong competitors. For example, Vertiv Holdings (VRT) is a large incumbent in this space, and they are not going to stop innovating better products. If SPXC product is not good enough, or clients still prefer to stick with a proven large incumbent, SPXC may not grow as fast as I thought.

Conclusion

I think SPXC deserves a buy rating. My main focus is the data center cooling part of the story, which no longer seems like a small side business. Olympus Max is giving proof that product traction is real, and SPXC is adding capacity to support the next leg of growth. If management executes well, I believe SPXC can grow into a more important player in this part of the market, and valuation multiples could move up.

data.title: Rebalancing For A Fragmenting World: Why Broad Commodities Still Matter
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

A Different Kind of Commodity Cycle

Commodity markets are often framed through a familiar lens: global growth accelerates, demand rises, and prices follow. But the current environment is being shaped by something far less linear and possibly far more consequential.

Today’s commodity cycle is increasingly defined by supply-side disruptions and geopolitical fragmentation, not synchronized demand strength. While energy markets remain central to the narrative, the more important developments are happening beneath the surface, in the inputs and linkages that tie the entire commodity ecosystem together.

One such linkage is fertilizer.

Significant geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, alongside broader dislocations in global trade flows, are beginning to affect fertilizer availability and pricing. This may seem like a niche issue, but fertilizer sits upstream of global agricultural production. When its cost or accessibility changes, it reshapes decision-making across millions of acres of farmland. Even if there is a ceasefire or deal, this cannot be instantly solved.

The result is a cascading set of second-order effects that markets do not always price efficiently or immediately. And it is within these second-order effects where we believe some of the most compelling potential opportunities, as well as risks, are emerging.

The Catalyst: Fertilizer Shock and Agricultural Repricing

To understand the current shift, it helps to simplify the mechanism.

When fertilizer becomes more expensive or less available, farmers respond rationally. They adjust crop selection toward those that require fewer inputs. In practice, this often means a relative shift toward soybeans, which are less fertilizer-intensive than crops like corn and wheat.1

This seemingly innocuous move then introduces new imbalances.

As more acreage shifts toward soybeans, the supply outlook for corn and wheat becomes more constrained. Even if demand remains stable, the risk of underproduction increases, tightening markets and creating upward pressure on prices. This is not a demand shock but instead a supply elasticity shock, where production cannot easily adjust to maintain equilibrium.

At the same time, other agricultural commodities are influenced by adjacent dynamics that are becoming increasingly important in today’s environment. Sugar, in particular, sits at a unique intersection of food, energy, and geopolitics.2

Brazil, the world’s largest sugar exporter, provides the clearest example of this dynamic. Its mills have the ability to allocate sugarcane between sugar and ethanol production in real time, based on relative profitability. When oil prices rise, ethanol prices tend to follow, creating a powerful incentive to divert sugarcane away from food production and toward energy.

But the story does not stop there.

Recent analysis suggests that this flexibility is being exercised at a meaningful scale. Brazilian producers are already increasing the share of sugarcane allocated to ethanol, with some estimates pointing to a majority of output shifting toward fuel production as ethanol prices materially exceed sugar prices. At the same time, global sugar balances that were previously expected to remain in surplus could quickly tighten, or even flip into deficit, if this allocation persists.

Layer on top of this additional constraints, and the setup becomes more compelling. Disruptions to Middle Eastern shipping routes, particularly through the Strait of Hormuz, are affecting refined sugar flows into key import regions. India, another major producer, is facing export limitations that further reduce available supply to global markets. Meanwhile, rising fertilizer costs are beginning to impact planting decisions and yield expectations, creating a lagged but potentially durable constraint on future production.4

What emerges is a commodity increasingly shaped by multiple reinforcing supply pressures:

  • Energy markets pulling supply toward ethanol
  • Trade disruptions limiting global availability
  • Policy and export constraints reducing flexibility
  • Input costs constraining future production

In combination, these forces highlight why sugar is not simply an agricultural commodity, but rather a cross-asset expression of tightening global supply conditions.

To be clear, this is not a call to “buy agriculture broadly.” It is a recognition that relative value within commodities is shifting, driven by changes in production incentives rather than consumption trends.

From Signal to Action: How We Are Positioning GCC

Translating these macro signals into portfolio positioning requires discipline. The goal is not to chase recent price performance, but to identify where future imbalances are most likely to emerge.

In that context, we have implemented a series of measured adjustments within the WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund (GCC):

  • Reduced exposure to cattle
  • Trimmed soybean exposure modestly
  • Increased allocations to corn, wheat and sugar

Each of these moves reflects a consistent underlying principle: positioning away from areas where outcomes appear more fully priced and toward those where supply risks may be underappreciated.

Cattle markets, for example, have been trading near elevated levels. At the same time, rising energy costs introduce potential demand sensitivity, as higher input and transportation costs can ultimately influence consumer behavior. Protein consumption is not perfectly inelastic, and over time, higher prices can lead to substitution effects. This creates a less favorable risk-reward profile at current levels.3

Soybeans, meanwhile, have already been beneficiaries of the crop rotation dynamic driven by fertilizer costs. While the fundamental story remains intact, incremental upside may be more limited as positioning becomes more crowded.

In contrast, corn and wheat represent areas where tightening supply conditions may not yet be fully reflected in prices. If fertilizer dynamics persist, the shift away from these crops could create a more constrained production environment, increasing the potential for upward price pressure.

Sugar adds a different dimension. We already noted its linkage to energy markets. In a world where energy markets remain volatile, this connection can amplify supply constraints and create the potential for asymmetric upside.

Across all of these adjustments, the guiding framework is consistent:

We are not reacting to price, but rather we are reacting to changing production incentives.

Built for Diversification: A Different Approach to Commodities

It is worth stepping back to consider how these adjustments fit within the broader design of GCC.

Many commodity strategies are, either explicitly or implicitly concentrated bets on energy markets. Oil often dominates index construction, meaning that performance becomes tightly linked to a single macro variable: the direction of crude prices.

At one end of the spectrum sits the S&P GSCI, which is a benchmark built on a simple and intuitive premise: weight commodities by their importance to the global economy. In practice, that means weighting by production. The result is an index that heavily reflects the scale of energy markets, where crude oil alone can represent a significant share of global commodity production.4

The logic is clean. But the outcome is less so.

A production-weighted approach implicitly assumes that economic importance should equal portfolio importance. That may be true for measuring the global economy, but it could be far less compelling as a portfolio construction framework. Investors don’t allocate to commodities to mirror gross domestic product (GDP); they allocate to commodities for potential portfolio diversification, inflation sensitivity, and exposure to differentiated supply-demand shocks.

This is where the Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM) has represented a meaningful step forward.

Rather than allowing production weights to dominate, BCOM introduces constraints, which means capping sector exposures and limiting concentration in any single commodity. The goal is to prevent energy from overwhelming the index and to create a more balanced representation across agriculture, metals, and livestock.5

That is a clear improvement.

But it still largely operates within a static framework. The diversification is structural, not adaptive. It reduces concentration risk, but it does not actively respond to changing market conditions, term structure dynamics, or the evolving opportunity set across commodities.

We think that distinction matters.

Because commodity markets are not just different, but cyclical, fragmented, and often driven by idiosyncratic forces. Energy responds to geopolitics and global growth. Metals reflect industrial cycles. Agriculture is shaped by weather, policy, and input costs. These forces do not move in sync, and they may not reward static exposure.

This is where more dynamic approaches, such as the WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund (GCC), begin to diverge more meaningfully.

Rather than simply redistributing weights, GCC incorporates signals, such as roll yield and momentum that attempt to selectively allocate toward commodities with more favorable structural characteristics. In doing so, it moves beyond the question of how much to own and toward the more important question of which exposures are worth owning at a given point in time.

In other words, put simply:

  • GSCI asks: What does the global commodity market look like?
  • BCOM asks: How do we make that investable?
  • GCC asks: How do we make it work for investors?

That progression, from representation, to diversification, to optimization, is ultimately what defines the evolution of commodity indexing.

The Case for Broad Commodity Exposure

For investors, the relevance of commodities extends beyond tactical positioning. At a portfolio level, commodities offer a set of characteristics that are particularly valuable in the current macro environment.

1. Inflation Sensitivity6

Commodities are often the first responders to inflation, particularly when that inflation is driven by supply constraints rather than demand overheating.

Unlike financial assets, which can lag or be impacted by policy responses, commodity prices adjust in real time to changes in availability and cost structures. In an environment where inflation is increasingly shaped by geopolitical and supply-side factors, this sensitivity becomes more important.

2. Exposure to Supply Shocks7

Commodities embed a form of optionality to disruption.

It is difficult to predict the exact nature or timing of supply shocks, whether they originate from geopolitical conflict, trade restrictions, or environmental factors. But a diversified exposure allows investors to participate in these events when they occur, without needing to forecast the specific catalyst in advance.

This is particularly relevant today, as supply chains become more complex and more vulnerable to disruption.

3. Diversification and Low Correlation8

Commodities have historically exhibited low correlation to traditional equity and fixed income assets, especially during periods of stress driven by inflation or supply constraints.

In scenarios where both stocks and bonds face pressure, such as stagflationary environments, commodities can potentially provide a valuable counterbalance.

From Oil-Centric to System-Aware Investing

The evolution of commodity markets is subtle but significant.

For much of the past decade, the narrative has been dominated by energy. Oil prices have served as the primary signal, and many investment strategies have been structured accordingly.

But the current environment suggests a shift.

Second-order effects, like fertilizer availability influencing crop production, are becoming first-order drivers of market outcomes. Agricultural markets are being shaped by energy dynamics, policy decisions, and input costs in ways that are more interconnected than in the past.

At the same time, global supply chains are becoming less efficient, more regionalized, and more susceptible to disruption. This increases the likelihood that idiosyncratic shocks within specific commodity sectors will drive returns.

In this context, a more system-aware approach to commodity investing becomes essential.

Rather than concentrating exposure in a single sector, the opportunity lies in recognizing how different parts of the commodity complex interact, and positioning accordingly.

The adjustments we are making within GCC reflect that philosophy. They are not large, directional bets, but incremental shifts designed to align the portfolio with evolving supply dynamics and relative value opportunities.

  1. Source: Hayden, M. (2026). Soybean vs. corn fertilizer: Key differences in application and use. ShunCy.

  2. Source for narratives on sugar, unless otherwise stated: Taylor, C. (2026, March 20). Sour strait = sweet sugar (The Cascade, Vol. 389). Pinecone Macro Research.

  3. Source: American Farm Bureau Federation. (2025). Economics of the U.S. beef and cattle market.

  4. Source: S&P Dow Jones Indices. (2025, October). S&P GSCI methodology. S&P Global.

  5. Source: Bloomberg Index Services Limited. (2025, January). The Bloomberg Commodity Index methodology. Bloomberg.

  6. Source: Diaz, E. M., Cúñado, J., & Pérez de Gracia, F. (2023). Commodity price shocks, supply chain disruptions and U.S. inflation. Finance Research Letters.

  7. Sources: Gorton, G., & Rouwenhorst, K. G. (2006). Facts and fantasies about commodity futures. Financial Analysts Journal; Hong, H., & Yogo, M. (2012). What does futures market interest tell us about the macroeconomy? Journal of Financial Economics.

  8. Sources: Erb, C. B., & Harvey, C. R. (2006). The strategic and tactical value of commodity futures. Financial Analysts Journal; Bianchi, D., Büchner, M., & Tamoni, A. (2021). Inflation hedging in the long run. Journal of Alternative Investments.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing including possible loss of principal. An investment in this Fund is speculative, involves a substantial degree of risk, and should not constitute an investor's entire portfolio. One of the risks associated with the Fund is the complexity of the different factors which contribute to the Fund's performance. These factors include use of commodity futures contracts. In addition, bitcoin exchange-traded products (ETPs) and bitcoin futures are relatively new and the markets may be less developed. They are subject to unique and substantial risks, and historically, have been subject to significant price volatility. As a result, the markets for bitcoin futures and bitcoin ETPs may be less developed, and at times, potentially less liquid and more volatile, than more established commodity futures and ETP markets. While the bitcoin futures market has grown substantially since bitcoin futures commenced trading, there can be no assurance that this growth will continue. In addition, derivatives can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities and more sensitive to the effects of varied economic conditions. The value of the shares of the Fund relate directly to the value of the futures contracts and other assets held by the Fund and any fluctuation in the value of these assets could adversely affect an investment in the Fund's shares. Because of the frequency with which the Fund expects to roll futures contracts, the price of futures contracts further from expiration may be higher (a condition known as “contango”) or lower (a condition known as “backwardation”) and the impact of such contango or backwardation may be greater than the impact would be if the Fund experienced less portfolio turnover. The Fund will not invest in bitcoin directly. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: Verizon: A Strong Buy Due To Its Resilient Cash Flow And Stable Valuation
data.description:
In September last year I had recommended a strong buy for Verizon Communications (VZ). I was honestly looking for value and defensive stocks at that time, expecting some cooling in growth markets. I had positioned Verizon as an income-first vehicle, with a supportive regime ahead for share prices. Any rerating-led upside was optional for me. Verizon has lived up to that expectation very well with a total return of almost 9% since my Strong Buy call, at a time when the broader markets moved by only ~5%.

Verizon's strength in choppy markets toward the end of 2025 and in 2026 was very much visible (likely helped by some rotation away from growth into value/defensive names). At some point the rerating had pushed price appreciation alone to well above double digit returns, although recent corrections have coincided with a recent rebound in growth stocks (following some de-escalation signs in the Middle East).

Even after the round trip, share prices are still up by 2-3% since the time of my earlier thesis, while income has again done the heavy lifting as expected. Verizon continues to grind its way, outperforming the markets, and I expect more of the same as the upcoming quarters see the broader markets consolidate at best as AI trades cool further (earnings grow into valuations, no further immediate rerating till capex cycles and AI economics become clear enough to bet on long duration assumptions, and stagflation risks are watched closely). However, total returns are not part of my thesis - it is not what Verizon is designed for. I continue to look at it as an income vehicle - share price appreciation is optional; support here is sufficient to keep the income-led Strong Buy alive.

A review of numbers and what is happening structurally at Verizon reveals that valuations remain stuck at ~9x forward earnings (no rerating yet). Free cash flow has stayed strong and looks good ahead. A strong buyback outlook looks supportive for prices. Verizon has also discussed a major reshuffle with an eye on resetting its growth strategy - fixing churn from past pricing issues, shifting to value-led growth, cost optimizations, lowering capex, and improving fiber–wireless bundling. That could mean suppressed pricing boosts in the short term, but a durable and higher-quality revenue base over time (leaning on customer lifetime value, retention, and bundling). The upside, therefore, is still conditional through this transition (revenue growth in the 2-3% range and wireless flat), but importantly, the downside appears well-protected. That's all that matters to retain the Strong Buy from the income lens.

Price - Upside, Downside and Most Likely Path

The valuation story has not changed since September 2025 and has been extremely stable over the past few quarters, with the forward PE ratio at ~9x on average (and over the past 5 years). The valuation is at a discount to the sector median, but that does not immediately signal a scope for rerating, unless growth returns. Revenue growth was reported at ~2% YoY in Q4 2025 and 2026 is explicitly positioned as a transition year.

Growth quality also appears weaker. Verizon is currently avoiding price hikes and is even absorbing promotions to address churn (still looks elevated). That is not only a soft signal for increasing competition but also means the revenue outlook in the immediate future is not high quality growth. Both EBITDA and EPS were slightly under pressure in Q4 despite subscriber additions - a signal that markets should not be expected to be in a hurry to rerate Verizon soon.

What we can argue for with more conviction than an immediate rerating, is a support to valuations, given that recent multiple compression has rarely been sustained, especially below ~8.5x earnings - in fact Verizon has stayed extremely resilient (often rerated) even under broader market liquidity stresses.

The cash flow and earnings story looks better poised even as revenue growth is not impressive. EPS is guided to be up by 4-5% in 2026. This is not through an improved demand or pricing environment, though. The primary drivers here appear to be cost savings (~$5b) and lower capex (~$4b lower than 2025 to ~$16-16.5b). Free cash flow has remained stable over the last few quarters and is expected to grow steadily by 7% in 2026 (guided at over $21.5b). With around $11.5b paid in dividends (based on numbers in 2025), there is sufficient residual cash to enable buybacks (at least $3b in 2026 and up to $25b over the next 3 years) and still leave behind some for debt reduction or reinvestments. The cost optimization and lower capex now boost EPS mechanically over the next few quarters and keep boosting FCF expectations till the transition plays out and growth strength returns.

The deleveraging trend that was still improving in September has taken a turn, and the debt-to-equity ratio has increased marginally in Q4. However, the debt-to-equity ratio may show weakening even if changes in the equity denominator (due to buybacks, retained earnings, or acquisition accounting). A better measure is to look at net debt to EBITDA, which is currently at ~2.2x. Frontier is expected to add ~0.25x near term, before a return to the 2-2.25x range by 2027 (through EBITDA growth and steady deleveraging). Overall, balance sheet risks are not elevated, neither deteriorating significantly to affect the thesis.

The overall take on the share prices is one of watchful waiting as the revenue and growth reset takes place. I expect current valuations and earnings to be fully supported, if not rerated or improved significantly. That support comes in non-structural form - cost cuts and capex clampdowns, and even buybacks. However, FCF looks healthy through the reset - sufficient to support capital allocation to both debt and shareholders. The onus of total returns shifts to income and income alone, as a result.

Income - Sustainable and Growing

FCF coverage for dividends has already been discussed and remains as strong as in September. The outlook for 2026 is also well supported, with FCF growth expected over an already strong FCF in 2025. Importantly, this is after factoring in Frontier integration and continued investment, which makes the coverage more credible. Income is likely to keep thriving - recent dividend growth of ~$0.07 translates to a 2.5% increase (close to the 5-year CAGR growth rate). Overall, as long as investors look at Verizon as an income asset, the growth reset phase should not hurt them - we have already discussed how downside risks are low for prices from here, while the income angle is as strong as ever. That is what helps retain the Strong Buy rating - on a very strong carry trade.

I will keep an eye on how the revenue reset works out over the next few quarters starting Q1 2026. In terms of headline numbers, I do not expect thesis-altering developments from leverage, revenue, earnings, or free cash flow. However, how convergence (fiber and wireless bundling) is playing out will be the starting point to gauge execution toward the revenue reset (which could unlock rerating over the long term). Churn for bundled users is an important metric that should be tracked in the quarterly call (even if qualitative views). Another important area to monitor is how cleanly the ~$5b cost savings translates into margins (without hurting growth) - any margin expansion alongside stable or improving subscriber adds there could indicate that EPS growth is sustainable rather than temporary.
